name: Balearic Catalan

allophone_rules.notes: Majorcan stressed [ə], the eighth vowel of mallorqui, continues Latin E-long / I-short and so is an ETYMOLOGICAL, not an orthographic, split: Catalan spelling does not mark it, so the words that carry it can only be listed, not derived (Wheeler 2005 2.3; Veny 1982 ch. 4; Recasens 1996). This word-keyed rule pins ONLY the stressed mid-front vowel to [e] in the closed lexical set the arbitration cites — tenen [ˈtənən], tres [ˈtɾəs], aquell [əˈkəʎ], feina [ˈfəjnə] (and their inflections) — while leaving every other process (unstressed reduction, o-raising, spirantisation) to run normally. It composes over the positional selection because the allophone layer runs after phoneme selection. The set is intentionally narrow: over-applying stressed schwa to every stressed e would be wrong, since many stressed e continue Latin E-short / AE and stay open [ɛ].

Balearic carries open-mid [ɔ] in these lexemes where the default gives close [o] (grosses [ˈɣɾɔsəs], taronges [təˈɾɔɲʒəs], vora [ˈvɔɾə]); unmarked in spelling, so listed (Veny 1982 ch. 4; Recasens 1996; the ca-x-balear-005/010/011 arbitration). /v/ is preserved (labiodental), so vora is [ˈvɔɾə] not [ˈβɔɾə].
Majorcan 'torrent' carries close [e] where the default gives open [ɛ] (torrent [tuˈrent]); lexically listed (Veny 1982; Recasens 1996; the ca-x-balear-011 arbitration).

stress.notes: Catalan accentuation per the Institut d'Estudis Catalans (IEC) norm (Gramàtica de la llengua catalana, ch. 3; Wheeler 2005 §3.1). A written accent (à è é í ò ó ú) always wins and marks the stressed vowel. Unmarked words are OXYTONE when they end in a consonant other than -s-preceded-by-a-vowel and other than -en/-in — including the consonant+s plurals (-ns, -rs, -ts, -nts, -ps …), which are oxytone because the plural preserves the stress of the singular (important → importants). A word ending in a falling DIPHTHONG (-ai -au -ei -eu -iu -oi -ou) is oxytone (remei [rəˈmɛj], correu [kuˈrɛw], avui [əˈβuj]) — the diphthong is one syllable, so the word does not end in a bare vowel. Unmarked words are PAROXYTONE when they end in a (single) vowel, in a vowel + -s (-as, -es, -is, -os, -us) or in -en/-in (semblen, parlin). The diaeresis ⟨ï ü⟩ marks hiatus/pronounced ⟨u⟩, NOT stress, so it is deliberately absent from marked_vowels (següent is [səˈɣwen], not [səˈɣwent] with stress on ⟨ü⟩). Proparoxytones always carry a written accent, so the unmarked default is paroxytone. The diphthongs list is the Catalan inventory of tautosyllabic vowel sequences (Wheeler 2005 §2.5; IEC grammar ch. 4): the falling diphthongs ⟨ai au ei eu iu oi ou ui⟩ and the rising ⟨ua ue uo ui⟩ after ⟨g/q⟩. Every other vowel sequence is HIATUS and counts as two syllables — te-ni-a, ve-ï-na, di-a — which is what puts the stress, and therefore the unstressed-vowel reduction, on the right vowel. CLITICS: cliticless_words carries the Catalan mots atons AND the Balearic salat articles derived from IPSE — es (masc sg), sa (fem sg), es/ets (masc pl), ses (fem pl), so/sos (after amb) — together with the preposition+salat contractions des (de+es), pes (per+es), as (a+es). These are unstressed proclitics; the engine must not stress them (the salat-article-clitic-stress arbitration class; Veny 1982 ch. 4; Recasens 1996).

notes: BALEARIC (mallorquí, menorquí, eivissenc). It descends from Old Catalan (ca-x-medieval) by way of the EASTERN settlers — mostly from the Empordà — of the 13th-century conquest, NOT from modern Central Catalan, and the two have innovated separately ever since (Veny 1982 ch. 4; Wikipedia, Catalan dialects). What it shares with Central it declares here on its own merits: unstressed ⟨a⟩/⟨e⟩ → [ə], deletion of word-final ⟨-r⟩, the fricative [ʒ] for ⟨j⟩/⟨g+e,i⟩, ⟨ix⟩ = [ʃ] and the clitic (atonic) function words. What it does NOT share with Central: (2) the deletion of an absolutely word-final stop in an ⟨-nt/-lt/-mp/-nc⟩ cluster: Balearic keeps it (molt [ˈmolt], camp [ˈkamp]), and the 4catac expert transcriptions confirm it (molt alta = [ˈmolt ˈaltə], with the stop, exactly as in Valencian, against Central and North-Western [ˈmol ˈaltə]). The final-cluster isogloss therefore runs Central + North-Western against Valencian + Balearic; it is NOT the Eastern/Western line. /v/ is preserved as a distinct phoneme across most of the archipelago and is inherited from the core. Modelled here per the arbitration (Veny 1982 ch. 4; Recasens 1996): (1) the Eastern raising of unstressed ⟨o⟩ to [u] (mallorquí xocolata [ʃukuˈlatə], euros [ˈɛwɾus], torrent [tuˈrent]) — see positional_graphemes ⟨o⟩; and (2) Majorcan STRESSED [ə], the eighth vowel continuing Latin ē/ĭ — an ETYMOLOGICAL split a grapheme rule cannot predict, so it is pinned lexically by the BALEAR_STRESSED_SCHWA word-keyed allophone rule over the closed cited set (tenen, tres, aquell, feina). NOT modelled, deliberately: (b) the Majorcan phrase-level TOTAL ASSIMILATION of a coda stop to a following consonant (aquests vailets [əˈkev vəjˈlɛv]).

sources.author: Wheeler, M. W.
sources.title: The Phonology of Catalan
sources.notes: The reference phonology of Catalan and the primary authority for this spec. Cited by SECTION, not page: §2.3 vowel systems and the unstressed reduction of the Eastern block (reduction is stress-conditioned; the Western block does not reduce); §2.5 the diphthong inventory; §3.1 stress and its orthographic marking; §5.1 the consonant inventory, ⟨c/g⟩ before a front vowel, ⟨x⟩ and word-final ⟨-ig⟩; §5.2 spirantization of /b d ɡ/ after a continuant and the pre-lateral gemination that blocks it; §5.3 final-obstruent devoicing and cross-word voicing assimilation; §10.2–§10.5 the post-lexical/phrase-level processes — final-rhotic loss, final-cluster simplification, nasal place assimilation and phrase-level lenition. Section numbers are given because the printed page numbers were not verified.

sources.author: Badia i Margarit, A. M.
sources.title: Gramàtica de la llengua catalana: descriptiva, normativa, diatòpica, diacrònica
sources.notes: Descriptive, normative, diatopic and diachronic grammar of Catalan; the cross-check used here for the consonant and vowel inventories, the stress rules and the dialect isoglosses. Pages not verified.

sources.author: Recasens, D.
sources.title: Fonètica descriptiva del català: assaig de caracterització de la pronúncia del vocalisme i consonantisme del català al segle XX
sources.notes: The descriptive survey of Catalan vowels and consonants across the dialects: the Western block (Valencian, North-Western) does not reduce unstressed vowels; Majorcan Balearic reduces ⟨a⟩/⟨e⟩ to [ə] and raises unstressed ⟨o⟩ to [u] like the rest of Eastern Catalan; the North-Western word-final ⟨-a⟩ = [ɛ]. Pages not verified.

sources.author: Veny, J.
sources.title: Els parlars catalans: síntesi de dialectologia
sources.notes: The standard dialectology of Catalan and the source for the isoglosses this spec encodes per dialect: final ⟨-r⟩ retained in Valencian and deleted in the rest; final-cluster simplification after a nasal everywhere but after a lateral only in the Eastern block; Western [tʃ] for initial ⟨x⟩ and [jʃ] for ⟨ix⟩; the lleidatà final ⟨-a⟩ = [ɛ]. Pages not verified.

sources.author: Institut d'Estudis Catalans
sources.title: Gramàtica de la llengua catalana
sources.notes: The normative grammar. Ch. 3 (l'accent) is the source for the stress block: the written accent marks the stressed vowel and always wins; unmarked words ending in a vowel, a vowel+⟨-s⟩ or ⟨-en/-in⟩ are paroxytone and all other endings — including the consonant+⟨s⟩ plurals — are oxytone; the diaeresis ⟨ï ü⟩ marks hiatus, not stress. §6.2 and the 'mots àtons' list are the source for the clitic function words (articles, contractions, atonic prepositions/conjunctions and weak pronouns) that carry no stress and therefore reduce. Cited by chapter; pages not verified.

sources.author: Alcover, A. M. & Moll, F. de B.
sources.title: Diccionari català-valencià-balear
sources.notes: The dialectal dictionary of Catalan; it records the pronunciation of each headword per dialect and is the authority for the lexical stressed-mid-vowel entries in word_exceptions (gos, tot, dos, molt are close-mid [o]; cos, pont, font, tres are open-mid). Catalan spelling does not mark the ⟨e⟩/⟨o⟩ close/open contrast in unaccented syllables, so this contrast is lexical and can only be listed, not derived. Cited by headword; the dictionary is not paginated online.

sources.author: Wiktionary contributors
sources.title: Wiktionary, the free dictionary — Catalan entries
sources.notes: Per-dialect IPA transcriptions (Central / Valencian / North-Western / Balearic / Northern) for the individual headwords listed in word_exceptions; each entry there cites GDLC, DNV or DCVB. Used as a convenience cross-check on the DCVB, never as a primary source.

sources.author: Ladefoged, P. & Maddieson, I.
sources.title: The Sounds of the World's Languages
sources.notes: Cross-linguistic phonetic reference for the segment inventory symbols used here.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Catalan phonology (Wikipedia)
sources.notes: Accessible overview of the vowel and consonant inventories, the Eastern/Western reduction split, spirantization, final devoicing and final-cluster simplification; useful as a cross-check on the primary sources above.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Catalan dialects (Wikipedia)
sources.notes: Accessible overview of the Eastern/Western split, of the 13th-century repopulation that carried Eastern Catalan to the Balearic Islands and Western Catalan to most of the Valencian Country, and of the isoglosses encoded in the dialect specs.

location.notes: Palma — the centre of the Balearic islands. [Wikidata Q8826 P625]
